fields.description:
## Why
Audit Reliability finding #1 (HIGH). Two clones can both claim the same open ticket (the write lock is per-clone). On merge the STATUS fork resolves deterministically (lower event UUID wins, `reducer/_processors.py` ~L145) and `assignee` resolves by HLC last-writer-wins — but **both agents were told CLAIMED** and proceed to duplicate work. The resolved fork is only a replay-time `logger.warning("PARENT_CHAIN_FORK_RESOLVED …")` (`reducer/_processors.py` ~L159-167), surfaced by no user-facing command. There is no two-clone concurrent-claim test (audit 7.1): `test_store_concurrency.py::test_claim_storm_one_winner` is single-clone.

## Design note (why detection is split into durable + best-effort)
A lost claim is only *observable to a clone* once the two clones' event logs have MERGED locally. `push.push_after_commit` is best-effort and may not merge at all (`sync.push=off`/`async`, unreachable remote, retries exhausted). So this ticket does NOT try to reinterpret a claim's success as "you definitively won cross-clone" — a successful `claim` keeps its EXISTING contract ("claimed locally; pending cross-clone convergence"). Instead:
- **Guaranteed mechanism (durable):** the reducer records every resolved STATUS fork into PURE derived ticket state, and fsck/show surface it — so a lost claim is *always* discoverable after the fact, whenever the merge eventually lands (via any sync/fetch).
- **Best-effort immediate signal:** the claim path additionally emits an exit-10 loss signal **only when the local (already-merged) log shows this clone's claim lost** the resolved fork. When no merge is locally visible it stays silent (no false "you won" is ever asserted); the durable surfacing catches it later.

## What / files
1. **Pure derived-state fork record** — `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py`: when a STATUS fork is resolved, in addition to the existing `logger.warning`, append the resolution to the ticket's DERIVED STATE, e.g. `status_fork_resolutions: [{winner_uuid, dropped_uuid, loser_env_id}]`. Pure/idempotent on replay — NO `alert_store`, NO `repo_root`, NO external I/O, NO cross-subsystem import in the reducer.
2. **fsck + show surfacing** — `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py`: read `status_fork_resolutions` from the reduced tickets and emit a finding via the existing `lines.append("<TAG>: …")` + `issue_count` pattern (e.g. `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED: <ticket> — concurrent claim/status race resolved (dropped uuid=<…>, loser env=<…>)`). `show` annotates the affected ticket from the same derived field.
3. **Best-effort claim-loss signal** — `src/rebar/_commands/claim.py` `claim_compute`, after `push.push_after_commit(tracker)`: re-read the ticket from the LOCAL store; if the reduced ticket now carries a `status_fork_resolutions` entry whose winner is NOT this clone's just-written claim event (this clone's env_id is the `loser_env_id`), return the ConcurrencyMismatch signal (**exit 10**) with a "claim lost on cross-clone merge" message. If no such entry is present (no merge visible yet), return success unchanged.
4. **docs** — `docs/concurrency.md`: add a short note documenting the surfacing behaviour (derived fork record → fsck/show + best-effort exit-10) under the fork-resolution invariant (I8).
5. **Two-clone regression test** — `tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py` using the `two_clones` fixture: both clones claim the same open ticket offline, then push/merge BOTH ways so each clone's local log contains the UNIONED events. Assert: (a) the converged `assignee` is deterministic and identical across both clones; (b) on the losing clone, the resolved fork appears in derived state and fsck emits `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED`; (c) the exit-10 loss path is EXERCISED — driving the losing clone's claim-loss detection against the merged log returns exit 10 (assert the exit code / ConcurrencyMismatch specifically, not an "either/or").

## Scope
In: `reducer/_processors.py` (pure derived fork record), `_commands/fsck.py` + `show` surfacing, `_commands/claim.py` (best-effort exit-10 on visible loss), `docs/concurrency.md`, tests. Out: changing the fork-resolution rule (lower-UUID-wins stays — docs I8); distributed locking; any side-effecting write from the reducer; forcing a synchronous fetch/merge into the claim path.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Resolved STATUS forks are recorded in PURE derived ticket state by the reducer (idempotent on replay; no I/O / alert_store / repo_root in the reducer) — asserted by a reducer/replay test
- [ ] fsck emits a `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED` finding and `show` annotates the ticket from that derived state
- [ ] The best-effort claim-loss path returns exit 10 when the LOCAL merged log shows this clone lost the fork, and returns success (unchanged) when no merge is visible — the two-clone test EXERCISES the exit-10 path explicitly (asserts exit 10 / ConcurrencyMismatch)
- [ ] New two-clone concurrent-claim regression test: deterministic converged assignee identical across clones + fork surfaced + exit-10 loss path exercised
- [ ] `docs/concurrency.md` documents the surfacing behaviour
- [ ] `make test` green
